narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, an LSAT score of 31, upper-middle family income, and your plan to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 60% chance that you would fail it on your first try—above the typical threshold that flags higher-risk applicants, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction stems from how your profile stacks up against patterns seen in past students, particularly focusing on a few key academic indicators that carried the most weight in the decision. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 5.5 and signaled to the model a dip in performance relative to peers who typically pass the bar. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, also well below the average of around 5.7, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum compared to successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.50—lower than the dataset average of 3.21—actually worked slightly in your favor here, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing, perhaps because it showed some resilience in a challenging undergrad environment. While factors like your LSAT score, family background, and full-time status played roles, these three academic rankings dominated the model's thinking for your specific case. In essence, your situation differs from typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger decile rankings that align more closely with bar-passing trajectories. This doesn't define your potential, especially as a determined Hispanic student navigating these paths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average of 36.7, strengthening your upper-level grades if possible, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your background. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your unique experiences could also help reframe your profile for future applications. With targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
